perturbation.original_text: As used in this letter agreement (this “Agreement”), the term “Evaluation Material” means all information regarding the Company, its affiliates and its and their respective businesses, technology, products, prospects and plans, a Transaction (including the fact that the Company or its affiliates may pursue a Transaction with you and the existence and terms of this Agreement) and other information furnished to you or your Representatives (as defined below) by the Company or any of its Representatives in connection with the Transaction, irrespective of form or medium of communication, whether prepared by the Company, its Representatives or otherwise and whether furnished on or after the date of this Agreement, together with all tangible and intangible embodiments and copies thereof and together with any and all analyses, compilations, summaries, forecasts, studies or other materials prepared by you or your Representatives that contain, are based on or otherwise incorporate, in whole or part, such information.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this letter agreement (this “Agreement”), the term “Evaluation Material” means some information, at the Company's discretion, regarding the Company, its affiliates and its and their respective businesses, technology, products, prospects and plans, a Transaction (including the fact that the Company or its affiliates may pursue a Transaction with you and the existence and terms of this Agreement) and other information furnished to you or your Representatives (as defined below) by the Company or any of its Representatives in connection with the Transaction, irrespective of form or medium of communication, whether prepared by the Company, its Representatives or otherwise and whether furnished on or after the date of this Agreement, together with certain tangible and intangible embodiments and copies thereof and together with any and all analyses, compilations, summaries, forecasts, studies or other materials prepared by you or your Representatives that contain, are based on or otherwise incorporate, in whole or part, such information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'all information' to 'some information, at the Company's discretion' and 'certain tangible and intangible embodiments', the contract introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of 'Evaluation Material'. This could potentially conflict with securities regulations requiring full and fair disclosure of material information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the omission of material facts necessary to make the statements made, in light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading. The modified definition of 'Evaluation Material' allows the Company to selectively disclose information, potentially omitting material facts, which could be construed as a violation of Rule 10b-5.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'all information' to 'some information, at the Company's discretion' and 'certain tangible and intangible embodiments'. This introduces ambiguity and allows the company to selectively disclose information, potentially omitting material facts. This contradicts Rule 10b-5, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which makes it unlawful 'to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading'. By allowing the company to decide what information is disclosed, the modified contract enables the omission of material facts, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: You agree to be responsible for any failure by your Representatives to comply with this Agreement (including, without limitation, any actions or inactions by your Representatives that would constitute a breach if such Representatives were original signatories hereto (other than with respect to paragraph 7 below under the heading “Non-Solicitation and Non-Hire of Employees”)).
perturbation.changed_text: You agree to be responsible for any failure by your Representatives to substantially comply with this Agreement (including, without limitation, any actions or inactions by your Representatives that would constitute a material breach if such Representatives were original signatories hereto (other than with respect to paragraph 7 below under the heading “Non-Solicitation and Non-Hire of Employees”)).
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'comply' to 'substantially comply' introduces ambiguity. Under contract law, 'substantial compliance' is a lower standard than full compliance and is undefined, which could be subject to legal disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-701 requires certain agreements to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Changing 'comply' to 'substantially comply' creates uncertainty about what constitutes sufficient compliance, potentially undermining the clarity and enforceability required by the statute of frauds for agreements of this nature.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'comply' to 'substantially comply' weakens the original obligation. NY General Obligations Law § 5-701 requires agreements to be in writing. By introducing the term 'substantially comply', the contract creates ambiguity about what constitutes sufficient compliance, which the LLM explanation argues undermines the clarity required for enforceability. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, each party hereby acknowledges that it is aware, and that it will advise its respective Representatives who receive any Evaluation Material or are aware of the discussions or negotiations regarding a possible Transaction, that the United States and Dutch securities laws prohibit any person who has received from an issuer material, non-public information from purchasing or selling securities of such issuer (and options, warrants and rights relating thereto) or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person (including, without limitation, any of either party’s Representatives) is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, each party hereby acknowledges that it is aware, and that it will advise its respective Representatives who receive any Evaluation Material or are aware of the discussions or negotiations regarding a possible Transaction, that the United States and Dutch securities laws discourage any person who has received from an issuer material, non-public information from purchasing or selling securities of such issuer (and options, warrants and rights relating thereto) or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person (including, without limitation, any of either party’s Representatives) is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'prohibit' to 'discourage' weakens the language regarding insider trading. This contradicts securities laws that strictly prohibit insider trading.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 makes it unlawful to use or employ any manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance in contravention of SEC rules. By changing 'prohibit' to 'discourage,' the contract suggests that insider trading is merely discouraged rather than strictly prohibited, which misrepresents the legal consequences of violating insider trading laws.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 2B / § 78j Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 78j : Manipulative and deceptive devices Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 2B-SECURITIES EXCHANGES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date Regulations §78j. Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'prohibit' to 'discourage' significantly weakens the stance against insider trading. Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as explained, makes it unlawful to use manipulative devices, and the law explanation highlights that weakening this language misrepresents the legal consequences of insider trading. 'Discourage' suggests a recommendation rather than a strict legal ban, which is contradictory to the legal requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 78j, indicates 'Manipulative and deceptive devices' are unlawful. The changed text does not reflect the legally binding nature of prohibiting insider trading activities, and thus contradicts the spirit and intention of the securities laws.

perturbation.original_text: The validity and interpretation of this Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York, without giving effect to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity and interpretation of this Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York, with full effect given to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Reversing the intent regarding 'conflicts of laws principles' could lead to a different jurisdiction's laws being applied, thereby changing the enforcement and interpretation of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law to govern their contracts, even if the contract has no reasonable relation to New York. The original clause explicitly excludes conflicts of laws principles, ensuring New York law applies directly. The modified text includes conflicts of laws principles, potentially leading a court to apply the law of another jurisdiction if the contract's subject matter is more closely connected to that jurisdiction, thereby contradicting the original intent of applying New York law directly.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that the laws of New York govern the agreement without giving effect to conflicts of laws principles. This ensures that New York law is directly applied. The changed text includes conflicts of laws principles, potentially leading a court to apply the law of another jurisdiction if the contract's subject matter is more closely connected to that jurisdiction. This contradicts the original intent of applying New York law directly. NY GOL § 5-1401, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows parties to choose New York law to govern their contracts, even without a reasonable relation to New York. By including 'conflicts of laws principles', the changed text undermines this choice by potentially deferring to another jurisdiction's laws, thereby creating a contradiction.
